data.title: Celsius Holdings: Fighting In A Challenging Environment
data.description:
Celsius (NASDAQ:CELH) is one of the companies I cover most frequently. On March 4, I published my latest article analyzing the company's performance in 2024. It was a very tough year that saw the stock drop from $100 per share to a low of $21. Since then, the stock has risen 30%, compared to a -3% return for the S&P 500 over the same period. Celsius has had a strong 2025 in terms of stock performance, but in this analysis, I will determine whether its fundamentals are showing the same strength.

Since I’ve written extensively about Celsius before—and have explained the details of the investment thesis more thoroughly in other articles—this one will be shorter than usual. I’ll focus solely on the Q1 results, as I don’t see any significant changes in valuation or risk compared to two months ago.

Earnings Review

Here are the figures for the quarter at first glance, and they look bad. However, the company already addressed this in their annual results. They acknowledge that Q1 has been somewhat slow, but they’re working on it:

"We are creating a ‘healthy halo’ campaign in 2025 to really solidify our position. We’ll launch it ahead of the summer beverage season. Additionally, more locations. Expanding product availability is key.” John Fieldly, CEO.

Analyst estimates had already reflected weaker expectations, but even so, the surprises have been quite negative.

But they say they’re gaining momentum toward the end of this very same Q1, so it’s likely that the first two months of the year were tougher than March and April.

This was reaffirmed during the earnings call. They plan to put more focus on promotions and aggressive marketing in the second quarter, since last year they concentrated those efforts in the first.

Let’s not forget that Celsius grew by +37% YoY in Q1 of last year, despite a negative sales impact from Pepsi inventory levels of between $20M–$30M. So, the comparable base was already a tough one. In the second half of the year, they also posted 23% YoY growth, again with a $20M–$30M drag for the same reason.

Even so, if they’re going to ramp up marketing efforts this year during the second half, we should see a return to positive sales growth after three consecutive quarters of decline. On the other hand, gross margin has reached a record high of 52.3%.

These are the reasons the company is giving, but the Essentials line was launched in the second half of the year, so I’m not sure what management is talking about…

"CELSIUS topline revenue declined 7% YoY due to early Q1 velocity softness, timing and structure of PEP incentives and retail promos, and lapping ESSENTIALS launch." Q1 2025 Investor Presentation.

What I do find concerning is the -10% drop in sales in North America, despite the +41% growth internationally. We’ll look at it later in terms of market share, but it’s clear that the Celsius brand is struggling. This likely has something to do with what Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) management mentioned:

"Alani Nu will probably continue to grow until they hit their ceiling on points of distribution. A lot of their growth is coming out of new distribution and they'll hit their ceiling on points of distribution. In fact, just like Celsius did." Hilton Schlosberg, Monster COO in the Q4 Call.

Celsius is already present in 98.7% of distribution points.

In any case, I’ve always said it’s better to focus on retail sales rather than the company’s reported numbers. And looking at the chart below, it seems to confirm what I mentioned earlier: “But they say they’re gaining momentum toward the end of this very same Q1, so it’s likely that the first two months of the year were tougher than March and April.”

February was the first month since May 2024 with positive sales growth, and it’s now been three consecutive months of growth.

In fact, it’s better to focus on this other table.

I already explained these two charts in the annual results analysis, but they’ve kindly included Alani’s sales this time. And by the way, we’ve got some fantastic news:

“Acquisition of Alani Nu closed April 1; brand surpassed $1 billion in retail sales in L52W1, reinforcing Celsius Holdings’ leadership in modern energy.” Q1 2025 Investor Presentation.

On the other hand, Alani is on fire. It’s the second-fastest-growing brand in terms of absolute dollar growth.

Thanks to this, the company as a whole has reached a 16.2% market share. I could say I’m surprised by Red Bull’s market share decline—but I already predicted this would likely happen. I explain it in the thesis update and the earnings review.

Conference Call

John starts by commenting on what I mentioned at the beginning of the article.

"We saw business fundamentals strengthen through the quarter and are encouraged by the positive momentum heading into Q2.Well, when you look at the first quarter, we got off to a slow start. And we have more of a balanced approach this year. We took some key learnings last year. We're cycling a lot of innovation in the first quarter. If you look at the Q1 2024, we had probably the largest -- we did have the largest distribution gains as well as innovation launching and great support and promo activity as well with those -- some of those lead launches." John Fieldly, CEO.

For the first time in the company’s history, they’re going to have a COO, and the background is promising.

"We appointed Eric Hanson, our first President and Chief Operating Officer in March. Eric brings nearly 3 decades of food and beverage leadership experience, including senior roles at PepsiCo. We believe that his experience will help us drive operational excellence, scale and unlock greater efficiencies in our partnership with Pepsi, our largest customer and North American distribution partner." John Fieldly, CEO.

I also like to see that key pillars of the original thesis continue to develop well.

"Food service continues to be a strategic growth channel for Celsius. In Q1 2025, we expanded into more than 1,800 Home Depot locations, increasing brand presence and everyday on-the-go consumption moments. We also recently began rolling out Celsius in 18,000 subway locations nationwide, a significant win that enhances both distribution and visibility during meal occasions. These new points of availability reflect our growing role in functional daily energy.

Foodservice now represents approximately 13.4% of North America sales through PepsiCo. And we see compelling runway ahead as we deepen our presence across work, retail and restaurant locations.

So the energy category has been kind of resilient on both dollar and volumes over the last several years. I know we had pressure in Q3 and Q4. But Q1 was very strong, starting to see growth rates come back." John Fieldly, CEO.

They don't expect any impact from tariffs, at least in the short term. They also haven’t experienced much cannibalization between brands—around 15%, which is lower than initially expected. Additionally, they are going to hold a specific call to explain more details and assist with modeling Alani Nu.

And perhaps this is what has managed to shift the sentiment around the stock price, which went from -12% in pre-market to being nearly flat at the moment.

"I mean the tailwinds look really favorable, especially after we get through really the next 3 to -- really the 4 to 5 weeks is kind of the peak revenue hurdles that you're seeing on a week-over-week basis or versus the prior year. Once we get into really June, you'll start to see some much easier comps that we'll have to -- that Celsius will be cycling. And the improvement in velocity that we've been seeing also is giving us strong conviction as we continue to build upon the consumer health and wellness trends that are out there. The category has come back to really strong growth." John Fieldly, CEO.

Conclusion

Overall, I think these are fairly lukewarm results. There are reasons to be optimistic—such as the self-inflicted damage in Q1 that could ease moving forward—but also reasons for caution, like the rising competition in the sector. I’ll continue to closely monitor the company, as I hold it in my portfolio and want to see how one of the most important years in its history plays out.

data.title: D.R. Horton's Undervaluation May Trigger Rich Upside Once Macro Headwinds Ease
data.description:
DHI Remains Undervalued Here, No Matter The Lowered FY2025 Guidance & Uncertain Macro Headwinds

We previously covered D.R. Horton, Inc. (NYSE:DHI) in April 2025, discussing why the stock was an even better buy after the selloff, thanks to the improved margin of safety from our fair value estimates and the double-digit upside potential.

Despite the risks arising from the inherently cyclical housing market and the higher for longer interest rates, we had believed that its investment thesis remained compelling then, resulting in our reiterated Buy rating then.

DHI 1Y Stock Price

Since then, DHI has seemingly bottomed at the $110s ranges before rapidly recovering by +15.2% by the time of writing, with a similar recovery also observed in the wider market and its home building peers in varying degrees.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the bottoming of market sentiments as many stocks entered oversold levels by April 08, 2025 and the new US administration announced the much needed 90 days tariff pause by April 09, 2025, with the reprieve triggering the upward recovery tide.

While the higher recessionary and/ or stagflation risks remain, it appears that the tariff war is already showing signs of de-escalation from both the US and China sides, as we near the July 08, 2025 deadline and market sentiments temporarily lift.

These may also trigger further recovery tailwinds to DHI's prospects, since the management has highlighted the unlikelihood of any material pricing impact until "the end of this fiscal year and into 2026," despite the "price increase announcements coming from suppliers" and the fact that "20% of the lumber that we use in our homes is (sourced) through Canada."

This is significantly aided by the fact that the company remains the largest homebuilder by volume in the US, with 13.6% in market share as of 2023 (+0.6 points YoY), allowing them "to leverage our relationships and our scale to navigate the cost environment better than smaller builders."

While these numbers may appear backward looking, given the uncertain impact on the tariff war on its FQ3'25 performance metrics, for now, DHI has also reported decent FQ2'25 performance by April 17, 2025, with home sales revenues of $7.18B (+0.5% QoQ/ -15.1% YoY) and adj EPS of $2.58 (-1.1% QoQ/ -26.7% YoY).

Despite the tougher YoY comparisons, readers must note the relatively volatile housing market for the past five years, with it underscoring why the homebuilding industry is entering correction years after double digit growths observed in FY2020, FY2021, and FY2022.

This is worsened by the still elevated 30Y Fixed Rate Mortgage Averages at 6.76% as of May 01, 2025 and the 15Y rate at 5.92%, up from the September 19, 2024 bottom levels of 6.09%/ 5.15% and the 2019 averages of 4.13%/ 3.28%, respectively, as the magic mortgage rates of 5% have yet to materialize.

On the other hand, readers must note that DHI continues to report growing net sales orders of 22.43K homes (+25.7% QoQ/ -15.1% YoY) with an estimated average selling prices of $374.38K (inline QoQ/ -1.9% YoY/ +26% from FY2019 levels of $297K), with it underscoring the robust demand for its new builds.

The same has been observed in its sequentially growing backlog to 14.16K homes (+28.7% QoQ/ -20.7% YoY) and the moderating unsold completed homes inventory to 8.4K homes (-19.2% QoQ/ +15% YoY), with it implying a relatively healthy consumer demand despite the macro uncertainty.

Even then, it goes without saying that DHI's investment thesis continues to carry risks, as observed in the drastically higher net debts at -$4.04B (-98% QoQ/ -40.7% YoY/ -112.6% from FY2019 levels of -$1.9B).

With the homebuilding industry being cash intensive, we believe that the company is likely to continue reporting heightened reliance on expensive debts at a time of elevated interest rates, with it likely to be a bottom-line drag in the intermediate term, as observed in the higher annualized interest expenses of $220.8M (+18.2% QoQ/ +9.3% YoY/ +57.4% from FY2019 levels).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, given the "record-high housing costs and widespread economic instability" along with the visibility into the potentially impacted early sales performance in April 2025, we can understand why DHI has already lowered their FY2025 revenue guidance to $34.05B at the midpoint (-7.4% YoY) and homes closed by homebuilding operations at 86K (-4.1% YoY).

This is compared to the company's original guidance of $36.75B (inline YoY) and 91K (+1.4% YoY) offered in the FQ4'24 earnings call, respectively, as Redfin (RDFN) also reports lower building permits ahead.

These reasons have naturally triggered the consensus' drastically lowered forward estimates, with DHI expected to report an impacted top/ bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +4.2%/+6% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +4.2%/ +6% discussed in our last article, respectively, with it underscoring why our caution surrounding the uncertain macroeconomic environment has played out as expected.

DHI Valuations

The lowered consensus forward estimates have also triggered DHI's relatively expensive FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.09x and FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 3.97x.

This is compared to the prior article levels of 9.95x/ 0.55x and the 5Y mean of 9.16x/ 0.47x and 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 11.87x/ 0.54x, respectively.

The same conclusion may also be derived after comparing against the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.43x and its homebuilding peers, including Lennar Corporation (LEN) at 3.12x and PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM) at 6.51x, with it underscoring why the homebuilding sector is likely to remain impacted in the intermediate term, DHI included.

So, Is DHI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

DHI 5Y Stock Price

Even so, we must highlight to readers that DHI has already lost all of its 2024 stock price gains while trading below its 100/ 200 day moving averages.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $13.21 (-10% sequentially) and the 10Y P/E mean of 10.40x, it is apparent that the stock remains cheap to our updated fair value estimates of $137.30.

Despite the consensus' drastically lowered FY2027 adj EPS estimates from the prior numbers of $17.08 to $15.24, there remains an excellent upside potential of +24.4% to our lowered long-term price target of $158.50 as well.

Based on the cash flow provided by operations at $2.86B on a LTM basis (+21.1% sequentially) and the management's FY2025 cash flow guidance at over $3B (+37.6% YoY), we believe that DHI remains well positioned to return great value to long-term shareholders, as observed in the reiterated FY2025 dividend payments at $500M (+26.5% YoY).

Its investment thesis is made even more compelling by the moderating short interest volumes by -9.8% from the recent heights, aided by the robust consumer spending growth by +1.8% YoY and the resilient US labor market in April 2025.

These developments signal a somewhat balanced near-term outlook for the housing market, despite the uncertainties surrounding the tariff war, the eventual impact on US inflation, and the still expensive borrowing costs.

With the worst of the pessimism likely baked in already, the CBOE Volatility Index also moderating, and the bulls defending the worst of the recent meltdown at the $110s ranges, we are cautiously reiterating our Buy rating for the DHI stock here.

We maintain our belief that this downturn is unlikely to last forever, with consensus upgrades rather likely once borrowing costs moderates, consumer demand returns, and market sentiments lift surrounding the cyclical industry.

For now, patience may be more prudent in the mean time.

data.title: Lattice's Power-Efficient FPGAs Offer A Niche
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lattice Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:LSCC) sits at the intersection of several fast-growing trends in the semiconductor space. Its ultra-low power small FPGA portfolio is well-matched to edge AI, 5G, robotics, industrial automation, and automotive. Field-programmable chips can be reconfigured for new tasks without expensive NRE. This is a key advantage for AI/5G/IoT, where workloads and standards evolve rapidly. In practice, Lattice FPGAs are being embedded as "sidecars" alongside CPUs/SOCs in cameras, networking gear, and instrumentation-handling specialized tasks that would otherwise tax the main processor. It is important to mention that the broader FPGA market is expanding quickly. According to SNS Insider, the FPGA market is expected to grow by a CAGR of 12% by 2030 as digital workloads increase. Accordingly, Lattice is becoming the "low-power programmable leader" in this space with a growing customer base and optimized FPGA solution stacks. The company's positioning across communications, computing, industrial, automotive, and consumer markets gives LSCC a powerful secular tailwind. I believe LSCC is positioned to gain a share of the TAM over time as markets like edge AI and industrial automation increasingly demand embedded, power-efficient intelligence.

For these reasons, I rate LSCC a buy with a price target of $80 over a 12-24 month period. I will discuss my rating in detail below.

Business Overview

Lattice is a fabless semiconductor company focused exclusively on FPGAs/CPLDs. It claims leadership in low-power, small form factor FPGAs. Some of its core products include the Nexus platform, the Avant series, and other families. However, I will focus more on the Nexus platform and Avant for its strong design-win momentum in the market. As we have seen, LSCC operates in a diverse ecosystem. From communications to automotive and consumer markets. In the FPGA space, Lattice Semiconductor stands out as a leader. According to Microchips US, LSCC has a carved niche as noted:

Lattice Semiconductor stands out in the FPGA market with its focus on low-power, cost-effective solutions. Unlike competitors that target high-performance, compute-heavy applications, Lattice has carved a niche in markets where compact form factors, energy efficiency, and affordability are critical. This strategy has established the company as a leader among the top FPGA manufacturers, particularly in consumer electronics, industrial automation, and automotive systems.

This is not the only thing that makes LSCC special. Its competitive positioning is also of importance. While it competes with Intel's (INTC) Altera, AMD's (AMD) Xilinx, and Microchip's (MCHP) Actel in the low-end FPGA, its 'low power, low latency, and small in size' is something that has greatly differentiated it from competitors. LSCC says that its chips can take 2-3x lower consumption of power than competitive chips.

1Q 2025 Earnings Review

Just recently, on May 5th, LSCC released its 1Q 2025 earnings report. The company reported $120 Mn of revenue. While this was generally flat compared to the previous year, it was in line with management guidance. Its non-GAAP (EPS) beat consensus estimates at $0.22. The company also generated healthy operating cash flows and used part of it to repurchase over 25 Mn of shares.

Key takeaways

Management highlighted a record design-win activity for its small/mid FPGA. This creates an interest in both general-purpose and AI-optimized server platforms. Specifically, industrial and automotive end markets showed growth, which was the first in 6 quarters. This suggests a stabilization after a previous slowdown. Guidance for 2Q 2025 implies that revenues will fall between $118 Mn and $128 Mn. During the release of the 1Q 2025, management also reaffirmed its full-year guidance. It also noted that it was experiencing a higher book-to-bill and a stronger backlog due to an increase in demand.

Growth and Profitability

Lattice has delivered strong historical growth by gaining traction with its Nexus/ECP5/MachXO families in key applications. Management targets a higher percentage revenue growth in the coming years, and this rests on continued uptake of Nexus 2 devices and the Avant series. The design-win momentum, especially in edge servers and vision systems, is cited as a leading indicator that should translate into higher shipments later in 2025 and into 2026.

Profitability is also improving. Gross margins have been stabilizing at about 68%, reflecting higher margins due to the nexus business and better utilization of fab capacity. I think free cash flow margins of about 18% are healthy for a semiconductor company. R&D costs are still high, taking about 35% of sales. However, this is necessary for a company that wants to stay competitive in FPGA and AI development. I believe that as LSCC focuses more on its flagship products, Nexus and Avant chips, there is a higher chance of margin expansion. Ideally, larger FPGAs have similar margins but higher revenue per unit.

Valuation

LSCC's current valuation reflects a mix of appreciation for secular growth and concerns over short-term cyclicality. On an EV/EBITDA basis, LSCC is trading at 68x. This appears fairly priced, relative to traditional peers. From a FWD PE perspective, Lattice trades at quite a high ratio of 153.5x against the industry average of 23x. This is quite high and as a result of its high growth premium.

With that, I think some premium is warranted if Lattice delivers on growth. Most of its peers in the FPGA space trade at 25x FWD (EPS). I think if Lattice can sustain high double-digit growth rates and be able to expand margins modestly, its fair multiple could meaningfully re-rate upwards. The bottom line is that LSCC's multiples look rich relative to the big players like AMD and Intel, and not very usual for a small, high-growth player. Nevertheless, I believe if LSCC delivers on its growth plan, the high multiples will begin contracting to fit the profile of a smaller player. As of the current multiples and valuation, it means investors are pricing in high growth and strong management execution.

Lattice Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for LSCC is simply that the stock's high valuation proves fragile. Any shortfall orders or delay in new product ramps could trigger a sharp multiple collapse. LSCC's EV/EBITDA and P/E are at record highs, and if semiconductor sentiment cools down, LSCC would likely underperform. Secondly, Lattice's end markets are cyclical. A sustained macro downturn could hurt capital equipment and consumer spending. The company itself highlighted that global semiconductor inventories remain elevated, meaning end-market pull-in could disappoint. If chip consumption does not pick up as expected, LSCC may face additional quarters of flat or down sales. And lastly, competition. This is a brutal space with big players and powerful incumbents. Intel's Agilex and AMD's Alveo are all targeting FPGA applications in data centers and 5G infrastructure. If customers favor those platforms, LSCC could lose market share. That said, Lattice's low-power focus gives it a niche, but competitive pressures on price or design wins could still impact Lattice.

Final Thoughts

Lattice is uniquely positioned to benefit from several long-term secular trends that favor its small, power-efficient FPGAs. Its gross margins and improving profitability offer a strong financial foundation. While near-term demand uncertainty and high valuation pose a risk, I believe these are outweighed by the growth runway. In my view, a sustained market downcycle or execution missteps would hurt LSCC. But any acceleration in end-market adoption would justify a higher stock price. I rate LSCC a Buy based on its favourable long-term growth and the prospect of multiple expansion if LSCC continues to hit design-win and margin targets.

  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Income Fund.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Update and Portfolio Review

  • Uncertainty about the direction of U.S. policy weighed on markets in Q1, as investors digested news on tariffs, cuts to government programs, and tighter immigration policy. U.S. equities fared poorly, especially large-cap growth and smaller-caps. Tariffs were a catalyst for the repricing of non-U.S equities. Non-U.S. markets' fiscally oriented growth narrative, cheaper valuations, and improved earnings estimates drove one of the largest relative-return differences between U.S. and non-U.S. equities in history. A falling U.S. dollar also boosted non-U.S. stocks. Fixed-income assets served as an equity diversifier amid near-term risks related to economic growth, inflation and the path of interest rates.

  • Fidelity's target date strategies produced positive absolute returns during Q1. Performance for the strategies as of March 31, 2025, is displayed below (net of fees)*.

Quarterly Performance for Representative Funds - Period Ending March 31, 2025

See the Performance Summary table in this review for performance reporting on all vintages. Funds shown in this chart are representative samples of where a target-date investor might be at various stages of their life.

Performance Attribution Summary

  • Active asset allocation decisions and the performance of the underlying portfolio managers contributed to the fund's performance versus the composite index in Q1. In particular, an overweight in non-U.S. equities and underweight in U.S. equities added meaningful value to the fund's relative result.
  • Among the underlying portfolios, non-U.S. equity portfolio managers, in aggregate, notably contributed to the fund's relative performance, as did U.S. investment-grade bond managers.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity funds with a value-oriented investment philosophy contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter. The largest fund-level individual contributors:

  • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund emphasizes companies for which long-term normalized earnings and free-cash-flow are underappreciated by investors. Sector allocations notably contributed to the fund's relative outcome, particularly an underweight in information technology. Overweight positions in GE Aerospace and Exxon Mobil (XOM) were among the fund's top individual relative contributors. Not owning benchmark stock Tesla (TSLA) also helped.
  • Fidelity® Series International Value Fund outpaced its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Value Index. The fund is managed by Alex Zavratsky, who believes that investing in attractively valued, high-quality companies (e.g., a strong balance sheet, stable return on equity and recurring cash flow) provides the best chance of outperformance in the long run. Outperformance was driven by security selection, primarily in the industrials sector, where an out-of-benchmark position in German auto and arms manufacturer Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF)(OTCPK:RNMBY) was the largest individual relative contributor. A non-benchmark stake in British-based defense and aerospace firm BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY) also helped, along with an overweight in Banco Santander (SAN).
  • Fidelity® Series Emerging Market Opportunities Fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. The fund is managed by a team of sector-based portfolio managers who specialize in security selection within their respective sectors. Security selection within the communication services, materials and health care sectors lifted the fund's relative result. Top individual relative contributors included an out-of-benchmark position in Singapore-based digital entertainment, e-commerce and digital finance conglomerate Sea, and overweight stakes in South African firm Impala Platinum Holdings (OTCQX:IMPUF)(OTCQX:IMPUY) and multinational commerce group PDD Holdings (PDD).

Relative Detractors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity portfolios with a growth bias generally detracted this quarter. The largest fund-level individual detractors:

  • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the health care sector notably detracted from relative performance for the quarter. The largest individual relative detractors included a larger-than-index stake in footwear designer and distributor Deckers Outdoor (DECK), along with semiconductor companies Nvidia (NVDA) and Astera Labs (ALAB).
  • Fidelity® Series Blue Chip Growth Fund trailed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Sonu Kalra, who invests in companies that he believes have above-average earnings-growth potential and a sustainable business model. Security selection within information technology, including overweights in Marvell Technology (MRVL), Astera Labs and Nvidia, meaningfully detracted from performance. An underweight and security selection in the financials sector also hurt.

Active Asset Allocation:

Active asset allocation positioning had a positive influence on the fund's relative performance this quarter, especially an overweight in non-U.S. equities and an underweight in U.S. equities.

Outlook and Positioning

  • The fund's glide path and strategic asset allocation reflect our long-term views and insights on participant needs, diversification and capital markets. Our investment process focuses on selecting strategic asset classes that provide appealing long-term returns, independent sources of return and risk, and favorable portfolio implementation attributes.
  • We maintain the view that trends in the economy and policy conditions may imply a regime change for financial markets. High debt, aging demographics, peak globalization, and geopolitics continue to create uncertainty in the path of inflation, policy and corporate profits.
  • With this in mind, strategic asset allocation decisions continue to emphasize diversification to help us navigate different risks. In recent years, we have amplified portfolio diversification based on a view that participants will experience multiple market environments throughout their lifetime, and that regimes can change abruptly.
  • Over long periods, equities have been a powerful asset for building wealth, while our fixed-income portfolio provides balance during periods of inflationary and deflationary stress. The fund's active allocation positioning reflects intermediate-term views on assets that we believe are mispriced relative to our view of fair value.
  • We expect the U.S. economy to muddle through tariff-induced challenges because of its large, diversified, services-based economy, with resilient companies and consumer-related sectors. We also acknowledge the considerable uncertainty about the outlook for the domestic economy, as investors, consumers and businesses assess the likely duration and impact of policy changes.
  • While non-U.S. companies are facing the risk of weaker demand from U.S. consumers, a number of factors may buffer the impact, including lower starting valuations and earnings expectations, fiscal room for governments to offset the impact of tariffs, and the opportunity to repatriate capital that has flowed into U.S. assets over the past decade.
  • We believe the range of possible outcomes is wide and the landscape is changing rapidly. This has caused volatility in both equity and fixed-income markets. We continue to assess incoming information as it occurs, in collaboration with analysts across Fidelity's multi-asset, equity and fixed-income teams. We continue to evaluate the fund's positioning, while adhering to our diversified, long-term approach.
  • In our view, heightened volatility and the persistence of uncertainty in the capital markets reinforce the importance of a disciplined investment process. Our target-date strategies are managed with a long-term investment horizon and provide investors with a diversified portfolio that seeks to balance their need for total returns with their sensitivity to many risks – longevity, market, inflation and deflation. During the 29 years we have managed target-date funds, this approach has proven to be a prudent and time-tested strategy. Since inception of Fidelity's target-date strategies, we believe we have achieved successful outcomes, as measured by total returns and by investors' tendency to remain invested throughout periods of volatility. We remain focused on researching the key drivers of outcomes for retirement plan participants and retirement investors, including capital markets, diversification and future expectations of participant attributes.

data.title: Amazon Looks Very Undervalued And Now A Deep Value Play
data.description:
The Q1 2025 earnings season is starting to wind down as every company in the Magnificent Seven has reported earnings except for NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). After going through all the earnings reports and looking at the revisions to the forward estimates, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) looks like a deep value play that may surprise the investment community. What's even more shocking to me is that AMZN is now the 3rd largest holding in the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV). Shares of AMZN have been correlated to growth for the longest time, not value, yet the combination of AMZN's forward earnings and current valuation is making it a deep value play in my opinion.

AMZN delivered a top and bottom line beat in Q1 and is trading at less than 26 times next year's earnings. AMZN can't keep up with demand for its A.I. services. There is tremendous earnings growth on the horizon, and AMZN just started the 2025 fiscal year generating an additional $12.35 billion in revenue and $3.1 billion in operating income compared to 2024. AMZN is trading at one of the cheapest valuations it's ever traded at, and I plan on adding to my position as I believe shares of AMZN are massively undervalued in today's market.

Following up on my previous article about Amazon

Back in February, I wrote an article about AMZN (can be read here) discussing why I felt shares were undervalued after they reported Q4 2024 earnings. Shares haven't lived up to my expectations as they have fallen by -19.61% compared to the S&P 500, declining by -7.31% since then on tariffs and trade volatility. I think that the selloff in shares of AMZN is overdone, as its focus on A.I. and cloud services during 2024 positioned it for increased future growth. I am following up with a new article to discuss Q1 earnings and why I believe the market is wrong on AMZN shares. I feel that AMZN is now trading in deep value territory, and we could see a big reversal over the summer as trade deals get announced and the Fed pivots.

Risks to investing in Amazon

While I am bullish on AMZN shares, investors should do their own due diligence as there are several risk factors to consider. If trade deals are not reached and tariffs are put into place, AMZN's costs could be increased, and margins could be negatively impacted. AMZN has $60 billion in debt on the balance sheet, costing them $2.3 billion in interest expenses. If the Fed decides that they need to reverse monetary policy and take rates higher, it would increase AMZN's interest expenses and compress margins, but it would also likely reduce spending throughout the economy. AMZN also faces significant challenges from Alphabet (GOOGL) and Microsoft (MSFT) in cloud as they continue to invest aggressively to gain competitive advantages on AMZN. AMZN also faces scrutiny over antitrust issues, labor practices, and data privacy, which could lead to lawsuits and result in potential fines and business model changes. AMZN is also beholden to a degree to global supply chains, and rising labor costs could negatively impact future earnings. Investors should do their own research because AMZN has several risk factors to consider, despite being one of the largest companies in the market.

Amazon is setting the tone with the offense as the Q1 results solidified a solid foundation for the remainder of the year

AMZN's Q1 earnings came in with a top line beat of $546.51 million and a bottom line beat of $0.23. This was led by YoY increases in revenue and profitability across all its lines of business. The North American segment grew its revenue by $6.55 billion (7.58%) YoY and its operating income by $858 million (17.22%) YoY. The International segment's revenue grew by $1.58 billion (4.94%) YoY while its operating income increased by $114 million (12.62%) YoY.

AWS continued to be a standout as its revenue grew by $4.23 billion (16.89%) YoY and its operating income increased by $2.13 billion (22.57%) YoY. Overall, AMZN increased the amount of revenue it generated by $12.35 billion (8.62%) and operating income by $3.1 billion (20.24%) YoY during Q1. AMZN is starting the year off with $155.67 billion of revenue and $18.41 billion in operating income. With AWS growing its operating income more than 20% YoY in addition to the other segments growing their operating income, AMZN is setting the stage for a strong 2025 fiscal year.

When I look at AMZN on a quarterly basis, I am excited about what I am seeing. AMZN just delivered its 2nd largest profitability across its operating and net income lines during Q1. Heading into the end of 2024 Q3, it set new records, then Q4 set new records again. Q1 2025 came in between Q3 and Q4 of 2024 as AMZN generated $17.13 billion in net income and $18.41 billion in operating income. On a YoY basis, AMZN's net income grew 64.19% ($6.7 billion), and its operating income grew $3.1 billion (20.24%). AMZN's margins are also improving as its operating margin increases by 1.14% YoY to 11.82%, which is its largest margin over the past several years. AMZN is outgrowing its expenses and increasing its profitability and margins, which is very bullish for the company's valuation as they could deliver larger top and bottom line beats in the future.

I think a lot of people are overlooking the fact that AMZN built an advertising business from scratch that is generating more than $10 billion in quarterly revenue because we focus on e-commerce and AWS due to how the business segments are reported. In Q1, advertising remained an important contributor to profitability as its revenue grew 19% YoY to $13.9 billion. AMZN is experiencing growth across its full funnel advertising offering, which helps advertisers reach a large audience and brands connect with customers. AWS has exceeded a $100 billion run-rate as it's a critical component to allow corporations to modernize infrastructure, lower costs, and speed up innovation. Now that generative A.I. is a new vertical, AWS could reach a run rate of several hundred billion in the future as the full potential from A.I. is still in its infancy. AMZN continues to invest aggressively in A.I. as it is building over a thousand A.I. applications across the company.

AMZN is utilizing A.I. internally to drive efficiencies in its fulfillment network and robotics while providing better shopping, Prime Video, and advertising experiences to its end users. AWS is constructing building blocks for external and internal builders to build their own A.I. solutions, which provide broad capabilities across every level in the A.I. stack. AMZN is also working on Bedrock, which is a fully managed service for leveraging frontier models to build Generative A.I. apps, which provides high-performing foundation models and features. AMZN's state-of-the-art model called Amazon Nova, which is part of Bedrock, delivers capabilities including Nova Sonic, which is speech-to-speech, and Nova Act, an A.I. model for web actions. AMZN will continue to be a pioneer in the generative A.I. segment as the A.I. business within AWS has a multi-billion dollar annual revenue run rate and continues to grow triple-digit YoY. The demand for A.I. workloads is consuming capacity as soon as it's added, and AMZN is investing heavily in infrastructure to support demand for these services, which should provide larger amounts of operating and net income in the future from their business verticals.

Shares of Amazon look extremely cheap as they trade at a better valuation with more growth than traditional value companies

Historically, AMZN was never looked at as a value company as it traded at elevated P/E levels for many years before pulling the lever on profitability. Companies such as The Coca-Cola Company (KO), Procter & Gamble (PG), Walmart (WMT), and Costco Warehouse (COST) were considered value companies due to their ability to mitigate the impacts of a recession and grow their dividends. Today, the tables have turned, and PG is the 6th largest company within SPYV and KO is the 10th largest holding. Big tech has taken over the value sector as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and AMZN are the top-3 holdings in this value ETF that has more than $24 billion in assets under management (AuM).

I think the market has lost its mind about how companies are valued today. When I look at the Magnificent Seven, PG, KO, WMT, and COST forward valuations, there is no reason for AMZN to trade at these levels. PG is trading at 23.21 times 2025 earnings and is expected to have 12.54% EPS growth from the end of 2025 through 2027, and trades at 20.63 times 2027 earnings. KO trades at 24.23 times 2025 earnings, and with 15.88% EPS growth over the same period, it's trading at 20.91 times 2027 earnings. Walmart and Costco, which are AMZN's largest competitors on the e-commerce side, are expected to have 25.57% and 22.48% EPS growth over the next 2 years and trade at 29.95 times and 45.68 times 2027 earnings. AMZN is on the cutting edge of cloud services and generative A.I. while revolutionizing e-commerce, yet it trades at a lower valuation than WMT, COST, and KO, while having almost an identical valuation to PG. AMZN is expected to generate $6.20 of EPS in 2025 and $8.95 of EPS in 2027. This is 44.35% in EPS growth over the next 2-years, and AMZN is trading at 20.67 times 2027 earnings. I think that AMZN is trading in deep value territory because there aren't many companies that can deliver more than 40% EPS growth over the next 2 years and trade at less than 21 times 2027 earnings.

Conclusion

AMZN's Q1 results have transformed it into a deep value investment opportunity, defying its historical growth stock persona. With a forward 2027 P/E ratio of less than 21 and projected EPS growth of 44.35% from the end of 2025 through 2027, AMZN trades at a compelling valuation compared to traditional value stalwarts like PG, KO, COST, and WMT. The large amount of growth across AMZN's business segments, coupled with a growing advertising business and A.I. initiatives like Amazon Nova and Bedrock, positions it for significant future profitability. I believe that AMZN will have a substantial rebound, and the current valuation makes them an attractive addition for value-focused investors seeking growth at a discount.

data.title: Booking Holdings: What Trade War?
data.description:
Being that we are in the midst of a trade war and a period of considerable economic uncertainty, it might not sound like the ideal time to buy a discretionary travel stock like Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG), but the long-term winner recently reported first quarter earnings and shrugged off any macroeconomic headwinds to once again post fantastic results. In fact, if you had been living under a rock for the last few months or are were awaking from a long slumber a la Rip Van Winkel, and for whatever reason decided to read BKNG's first quarter earnings report to catch yourself up on what's going on in the world, you'd hardly realize that a trade war, escalating tariff tensions, and talk of a recession are swirling.

I’m bullish on Booking Holdings based on its excellent recent results during a challenging time for businesses, its history as a long-term outperformer, and its attractive combination of dividend growth and share buybacks.

Company Introduction

Booking Holdings is the parent company of travel and accommodation booking platforms like its namesake Booking.com, Priceline, KAYAK, and additional subsidiaries. Founded in 1997 and based in Norwalk, Connecticut, the company has grown to a market cap of nearly $170 billion over the years.

Recent Results

If you only looked at Booking Holdings’ Q1 results, you’d hardly notice there is a trade war and talk of a recession going on. The company increased revenue by an impressive 8% year-over-year, despite these potential headwinds. Not only that, but room nights booked on the platform grew 7% year-over-year to 319 million (the first quarter in which the company surpassed 300 million room nights) while gross bookings also grew by 7% to $46.7 billion.

Meanwhile, the asset-light business is a free cash flow machine that generated $3.2 billion in free cash flow during the quarter, representing a 23% year-over-year increase.

If a travel booking company like BKNG can post strong growth like this during a time when travel demand faces considerable challenges, that’s the type of company investors want to own over the long term.

Share Buybacks

Booking Holdings has a long and proud history of decreasing its share count over time and returning capital to its shareholders through share buybacks. Share buybacks can be beneficial to shareholders because they reduce the number of shares outstanding and thus increase earnings per share over time, concentrating the company’s earnings among a smaller number of shareholders. They can also be viewed as a sign that management views its shares of its stock as undervalued.

During the first quarter, the company bought back $1.8 billion worth of shares. BKNG still has $25.9 billion remaining on its share repurchase program as of March 31, meaning that the company can continue to buy back a significant amount of shares going forward.

New Dividend Stock

Booking Holdings is finally a dividend stock after initiating a dividend payment in 2024.

While its yield of 0.7% may not sound like much to get excited about yet, it’s already off to a good start as a dividend growth stock, raising its quarterly dividend payment from $8.75 in 2024 to $9.60 for the first quarter of 2025.

Additionally, BKNG’s dividend payout ratio is just 18.7%, so there’s plenty of room for the company to continue to increase its dividend payout going forward.

The combination of a shrinking share count thanks to BKNG’s generous share buybacks and growing dividend payments should continue to make BKNG a winning proposition for investors for many years to come.

Valuation: A Slight Premium

Booking Holdings is an incredible performer firing on all cylinders and returning capital to shareholders. My only potential qualm is that its valuation is on the higher end -- shares trade for 24.2 times 2025 earnings estimates, which is slightly higher than the broader market -- the S&P 500 currently trades for 20 times NTM earnings estimates. However, this isn’t an excessive valuation and I’m happy to pay a slight premium to the broader market for a great company like Booking Holdings.

Long-term Winner

Another reason that Booking Holdings is a great company and deserving of a small premium is that its strong results are nothing new -- it is a long-term winner that has generated excellent returns for its shareholders over the years. Over the past decade, BKNG has generated a total return of 315%, outperforming the broad market SPY ETF (SPY), which returned 221% over the same time frame. Zooming further out, BKNG has returned an incredible 2,025% over the past 15 years, compared to 535% for SPY. Lastly, looking back 20 years, BKNG has posted a phenomenal 20,240% total return, versus a return of 600% for SPY. Past performance is of course no guarantee of future results, and I obviously don't expect a mega-cap stock like this to return another 20,240% over the next 20 years, but long-term winners tend to keep winning and BKNG is a prime example of this.

Risks

Booking Holdings is firing on all cylinders, but all investment opportunities come with risks so it's worth exploring what they could be in this case. A global economic slowdown or geopolitical tensions that put a damper on travel demand could be risks for a travel booking company, but based on BKNG's strong performance this past quarter, the company seems well-positioned to navigate these risks. Remember, the company achieved record room nights during a quarter when these fears were front and center for many consumers.

Longer-term, an additional risk some pundits have brought up the risk posed by agentic AI -- consumers may increasingly ask AI agents to plan and book trips for them. However, I am not overly concerned about this risk as Booking Holdings is at the forefront of incorporating AI tools into its own offerings for both consumers and providers. On the first quarter conference call, CEO Glenn Fogel reported that he is "excited about our continued work to incorporate AI technology in multiple ways across our platforms, which will continue to position us well for the long-term."

Investor Takeaway

I’m bullish on Booking Holdings. The company continues to post stellar results, increasing revenue, total bookings, total room nights and free cash flow even during trying economic times when its core business should be facing challenges. This is the sign of an elite company that investors can buy and hold for the long term. Furthermore, I like BKNG’s compelling combination of dividend growth and extensive share buybacks; a winning formula that should give investors further strong performance going forward.

data.title: CSX: A Strong Player In A Changing Freight Market
data.description:
Introduction

CSX Corporation (NASDAQ:CSX) is one of the pillars of the American railroad industry. The company operates a network covering the eastern United States, connecting key industrial regions with more than 70 ports, and plays a critical role in the transportation of raw materials and goods.

The year 2024 was a test of strength for CSX: the collapse of a bridge in Baltimore and several major hurricanes hit their network. However, the company managed to cope with the challenges: revenue fell by only 1%, while the operating margin remained high at 36.1%. New CEO Joe Hinrichs, who joined the company in 2022, has only strengthened the strategic vector for growth, especially focusing on the role of railways in reducing emissions and unloading road infrastructure. CSX is no longer just a railway operator, but one of the key drivers of logistics on the East Coast of the United States.

Business model

CSX's clients include major industrial corporations, agricultural producers, construction companies, and retail chains. CSX offers transportation of both bulk and bulk cargoes such as coal, metals, chemicals or agricultural raw materials, as well as container transportation. The business model looks like this: the company earns on tonne-miles, that is, it receives income depending on the weight and distance of cargo movement. Fuel surcharges become an additional element of pricing, which makes it possible to partially hedge the risks of fluctuations in fuel prices.

CSX's revenue structure is divided into two main categories. Approximately two thirds of the revenue comes from the transportation of conventional goods combined in the Merchandise Freight segment. These are construction materials, agricultural products, automobiles, chemicals and metals. The remaining part is formed by intermodal transportation — the movement of containers and trailers within the framework of complex logistics chains. It is in intermodal transportation that CSX management sees one of the points of business growth today. Despite the fact that coal transportation still provides the company with a significant share of profits, their contribution has been gradually decreasing in recent years following the general decline of the coal industry in the United States.

From an operational point of view, CSX demonstrates a deep adaptation to the requirements of modern logistics. The key step was the implementation of the principles of Precision Scheduled Railroading. This means that each operation is scheduled and optimized: fewer idle wagons, longer trains, higher loading of locomotives. As a result, there has been a significant reduction in personnel, maintenance, and infrastructure costs.

At the same time, CSX has maintained a multi-modal approach to business, actively developing the transshipment infrastructure. The construction of modern terminals has made it possible to integrate rail and road transportation, ensuring the delivery of the "last mile" — that very last part of the cargo journey to the end user. This has strengthened CSX's competitive position against traditional trucking operators, especially in segments where speed and flexibility of delivery are critical.

CSX's business model has undergone a major evolution in recent years. Previously, the focus was on the volume and quantity of tons transported, but today the focus has shifted towards profitability and efficiency. The company consistently reduces the share of low-margin cargoes, such as local coal for thermal power plants, and increases the volume of transportation of more profitable goods: chemicals, metals, bulk cargo and containers. This not only increases the average margin for transportation, but also makes the business more resilient to industry shocks and changes in demand patterns.

At the strategic level, CSX is becoming increasingly established as an integrator of logistics solutions in the eastern United States. By combining the rigorous discipline of PSR, multi-modal services and investments in digitalization, the company strives to take a strong place in the changing ecosystem of freight transportation, where those who know how to offer the client not just cargo transportation, but a full-fledged logistics solution benefit.

Competitors

The freight railway industry in North America is characterized by a high level of concentration: six major (Class I) railroads operate in the United States, and together with Canadian carriers (including the newly formed CPKC after the merger of CN and KCS) they dominate the market. By 2024, the market volume is estimated at $80-85 billion per year, and by 2030, analysts at Verified Market Research predict its growth to $128 billion at an average annual rate of about 4%. However, growth in the United States is expected to be more restrained — about 2-3% per year, due to high competition from trucking companies and a gradual change in the structure of the economy.

Within this market, CSX occupies one of the leading positions in the eastern United States, competing primarily with Norfolk Southern. In the west and south, Union Pacific and private BNSF dominate, while Canadian carriers CN and CP (after merging with KCS) partially overlap with CSX in the segment of eastern ports. In addition, CSX is facing pressure not only from rail competitors, but also from road transport: according to the US Department of Transportation, railroads handle only about 28% of domestic freight turnover, while motor vehicles account for almost 68%. Competition is particularly intense in goods such as chemicals and consumer goods, where trucks are more efficient over short distances.

According to my calculations, given that CSX currently generates about $14.5 billion in revenue, its market share in terms of revenue (including the share in North America) can be estimated at about 20-25%. The geographical location gives the company a strategic advantage: Its network serves the transit cargo of the East coast and ports of the Gulf of Mexico, and CSX demonstrates a strong position in its segment of the map in key commodity flows such as coal, oil, grain and automobiles. Unlike the base metals market in the Upper Midwest, where Union Pacific (UNP) and BNSF are more dominant, CSX and Norfolk Southern actually share the main cargo flows in the eastern United States.

Against the background of all these processes, the new tariff policy of the Donald Trump administration, announced in April 2025, is putting additional pressure on the industry. The increase in import duties on a wide range of goods will weaken international trade flows, which will inevitably affect demand for rail transportation. Intermodal transportation and exports of raw materials such as coal and agricultural products, which traditionally accounted for 35-40% of the revenue of large railway companies, will be particularly affected. In the first days after the tariff announcement, CSX shares adjusted down by almost 6%, reflecting market concern about the prospects for lower traffic volumes. The Association of American Railroads has also publicly warned of possible disruptions to passenger and freight traffic. In practice, this means the risk of closing a number of import logistics chains, which will lead to a reduction in trains to East Coast ports, primarily those served by CSX. Possible compensating effects, such as an increase in industrial production in the United States in the event of production localization, do not yet look large enough to neutralize the negative impact.

Financials

CSX's revenue in 2024 was $14.54 billion (-1% YoY), and the 4% drop in the fourth quarter was attributed to lower fuel surcharges and a drop in coal shipments amid weakness in this segment. Overall, traffic volumes remained stable, while intermodal transportation and tariff increases in other segments partially offset the negative. In the first quarter of 2025, revenue decreased by another 7% year-on-year due to the continued decline in coal shipments and temporary network outages related to infrastructure work.

The long-term revenue dynamics remains stable: over the past five years, the CAGR has been about 4%. I expect a gradual stagnation of revenue in 2025 (about $14.57 billion) and the beginning of a recovery in 2026. Growth will be supported by the recovery in industrial demand, the expansion of intermodal transport and the stabilization of fuel prices, while a slight slowdown in the decline in the coal segment is expected due to increased exports and a cold winter. My forecast is close to the consensus forecast, which expects revenue in 2025 in the range of $13.80-14.49 billion.

The company's EBITDA was about $7.07 billion, which is close to the level of the previous year, despite the overall drop in revenue, as fuel savings and cost optimization were partially offset. In the first quarter of 2025, EBITDA showed a slight decrease, following sagging revenues. I expect that with the recovery of industrial volumes and further cost optimization, CSX will be able to return operating margins to the 39-40% level typical for the end of 2022. My model envisions an EBITDA margin growth of up to 51% by 2025-2026, due to high fuel efficiency, improved train loading, and moderate fare increases. Although inflation and rising wages may put some pressure on the cost structure, I believe CSX has all the resources to maintain its EBITDA margin at 48%-50% or higher in the coming years.

The debt burden on the business remains moderate, with a debt-to-equity ratio of about 2.4x and Debt/EBITDA of about 2.7x, which is considered a healthy level for the capital-intensive railway industry. The interest coverage ratio remains at 6.5x, which confirms the company's comfortable ability to service its obligations through operating profit. The main part of the debt is bond loans with low coupon rates of 2-3%. Taking into account large-scale share repurchase programs, the capital structure can be gradually adjusted to reduce the debt share.

The company is actively modernizing tracks, bridges, alarm systems and the locomotive fleet, which is why CapEx has been growing rapidly in recent years. In 2024, capital expenditures increased to $2.53 billion, which is approximately 17% of revenue. This growth is due to the active expansion of network capacity and the renewal of rolling stock after the consolidation period. I expect that as large infrastructure projects are completed, CapEx's growth rate will begin to slow down: by 2026-2027, the ratio of capital expenditures to revenue will gradually decrease to 15-16%. At the same time, CSX will continue to actively invest in traffic control automation and fleet modernization over the next 2-3 years, maintaining the CapEx level at the level of $2.2–2.5 billion annually.

Against the background of rising capital expenditures in 2024, CSX generated about $3.35 billion in unlevered FCF, which is slightly lower than in 2023. I expect the flow to exceed $3.8 billion in 2025 and $5 billion per year by 2030. This growth will be supported by both improved operating profits due to the recovery in industrial demand and the stabilization of CapEx amid the completion of large-scale investment projects.

In fiscal year 2024, the company paid dividends of $883.6 million or $0.47 per share with a yield of about 1.72%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $958.8 million in dividends or $0.51 per share with a yield of 1.80%. In addition to dividends, CSX is implementing a large-scale buybackback program: in October 2023, a new $5 billion program was approved, according to which 95 million shares worth $3.17 billion have already been repurchased by the end of the first quarter of 2025.

Valuation

The company's shares have fallen by 15.47% over the past year, while the S&P500 index has grown by 9.07%. As a result, CSX shares have been undervalued and the price has increased by 15-16%, which can be used to buy a stable, well-established business with high cash flows and stable dividends.

Firstly, the company is trading at discounts compared to historical average multiples: the average P/E over the past 5 years is 14.9% higher than the current P/E at 16.93x, and the average EV/EBITDA is 14.7% higher than the current one. P/S and P/B discounts also remain close to historical averages and medians.

Compared to peers, the company also trades at discounts to the average values in terms of P/E, P/S, EV/EBITDA multipliers, as can be seen in the table below: for P/E, the discount is 22.6%, for EV/EBITDA – 22.9%, and for P/S, the discount to the average is about 21.1%. Thus, there is a clear underestimation of the company's shares in comparison with peers.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), target value of P/E, P/S and EV/EBITDA with estimated weights of 20%. In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 6.68%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. To keep the estimate conservative, I also added 0.5% as a specific risk in the CAPM calculation. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 17.22x, P/S is 3.75x, and EV/EBITDA is 11x (I took the consensus forecast).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $33.11 with the potential for a 16.31% share price increase. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 18.10%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating. My forecast is similar to that of Wall street analysts – the consensus expects a price of $32.81 with an upside of about 15.24%.

Key risks

A slowdown in U.S. industrial production or deepening trade conflicts, especially in the context of new tariffs under Trump's policy, could significantly reduce transportation volumes, especially in intermodal and export-oriented segments. The weakness of the coal market, which continues to make a significant contribution to revenue, creates an additional source of pressure. Increased competition from short- and medium-range trucking companies, especially in the context of lower fuel prices, can also pull cargo off railway networks. Rising inflationary costs — primarily wages and the cost of materials — could potentially eat into operating margins, despite efforts to optimize costs.

Key growth drivers

The recovery of industrial activity in the United States, especially against the background of a possible localization of production and an increase in demand for American goods, creates the basis for an increase in cargo flows. The expansion of intermodal transportation, where CSX holds a strong position, allows the company to capture additional market shares through hybrid rail + vehicle solutions. Network optimization based on Precision Scheduled Railroading and investments in digitalization ensure a steady increase in efficiency and margins. Finally, the large-scale modernization of the infrastructure, which is already nearing completion, will free up resources for accelerated profit growth in the medium term.

Conclusion

Despite stagnating revenue, the company maintains high profitability, generates stable free flow, and manages capital wisely. I believe that after the stock price correction by 15%, there was a slight underestimation, which can be used to buy a stable, well-established business with high cash flows and good dividends. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $33.11 with the potential for a 16.31% share price increase. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 18.10%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating.

  • Unconstrained by target asset allocation profiles or benchmark weights, the fund dynamically pursues attractive income and value opportunities across asset classes, while closely monitoring interest rate, equity and credit risk. This flexibility helps the fund adapt to market conditions, with the goal of generating capital appreciation in rising markets and mitigating losses in declining markets.
  • The fund brings Fidelity's specialists in income investing together in a single portfolio. The lead manager and co-managers work collaboratively to identify the optimal combination of securities, reflective of market conditions, in seeking to balance income potential versus risk. Each manager leverages specialized research teams to find the most compelling investment ideas within their respective areas of expertise.

Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance stated. Performance shown is that of the fund's Retail Class shares (if multiclass). You may own another share class of the fund with a different expense structure and, thus, have different returns. To learn more or to obtain the most recent month-end or other share-class performance, visit fidelity.com/performance, institutional.fidelity.com, or 401k.com. Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Review

U.S. stocks returned -4.27% first past three months, according to the S&P 500® index, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs.

On January 29, the Federal Reserve paused following three consecutive rate cuts beginning in September, extending its wait-and-see phase and indicating further reductions would depend on "real progress on inflation" or unexpected weakness in labor.

The central bank affirmed this stance at its March 19 meeting, when it held steady its benchmark federal funds rate as it assessed sweeping policy changes and the effect on the economy.

About a week later, new data showed that inflation climbed 2.8% in February from a year earlier – higher than the Fed's 2% target, more than economists expected and exceeding January's annual pace – as underlying price pressure intensified.

Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers slipped, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead. After gaining 25.02% in 2024, the S&P 500® rose 2.78% in January, as investors reacted favorably to the definitive election outcome and potential policy changes, including the prospect of tax cuts, expansionary fiscal policy and lower regulation.

Post-election momentum lost steam in mid-February, as the index returned -1.30% for the month. The downtrend steepened in March (-5.63%), when the index slid into a correction by declining 10% from its February peak.

Meanwhile, U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, gained 2.78% in Q1. The 10-year Treasury yield fell by 35 basis points (0.35%), bringing the nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yield down to 4.23%.

Historically, U.S. Treasury yields tend to drop when economic uncertainty rises – and uncertainty rose sharply in Q1 amid oft-changing plans for the implementation of new tariffs.

Against a backdrop of falling yields, fixed-income categories with greater rate sensitivity enjoyed the best price gains. Long-term (10+ year) debt issues outpaced intermediate- and short-term issues. By credit rating, bonds rated AA had a notable performance advantage over highest-quality AAA bonds and lower-quality investment-grade securities rated A and BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB).

Looking beyond the Bloomberg index for the three months, emerging-markets debt gained 2.35%, while U.S. corporate high-yield bonds (+0.94%), floating-rate loans (+0.48%) and preferred stocks (+0.08%) slightly rose. Convertible securities, however, lost ground, returning -2.14%.

Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index Global, Investment-Grade Debt - Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares returned -2.01%, trailing the -0.74% result of the Composite index, a 50/50 blend of the S&P 500® index and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index.

Security selection in the fund's convertibles sleeve was the largest detractor from performance versus the Composite index in Q1. Our holdings returned -10.79%, widely trailing the -2.14% result of the category benchmark. Convertible positions in Core Scientific (CORZ) and MicroStrategy (MSTR) were notable laggards this period.

Another detractor was the fund's significant underweight in investment-grade bonds, given that the category topped the Composite index by about three percentage points. However, this underperformance was tempered by security selection in the category, as the fund's overweight in longer-duration bonds helped, given these securities' strong result for the quarter.

Meanwhile, security selection in the equity sleeve modestly detracted versus the Composite index. The fund's stock holdings returned -4.81% in Q1, trailing the S&P 500® index by 0.53 percentage points. Individual stocks that notably hurt relative performance included non-Composite stakes in TransAlta (TAC) (-34%), Scorpio Tankers (STNG) (-24%) and Byrna Technologies (BYRN) (-42%). In contrast, a non-Composite position in DHT Holdings (DHT) and a large underweight in Nvidia (NVDA) (-20%) contributed.

Also on the positive side, the fund's underweight in equities – which often reflects our ownership of asset classes outside the Composite index – contributed, as did non-Composite exposure to high-yield bonds and floating-rate loans. The fund's cash position of about 2%, on average, also helped.

Outlook and Positioning

In managing the fund, we seek opportunities in a wide variety of assets, caused by temporary mispricing as investors overreact to fear or uncertainty.

We want to "do more with less," meaning we rely on Fidelity's skill and research to try to achieve a total return close to the historical average of stocks, but with much less downside risk.

The past three months, we stuck to our focus on generating total return for the fund by capturing the highest income possible, while simultaneously limiting equity, credit and interest-rate risk within certain asset allocation parameters.

The biggest change we made was to reduce the fund's exposure to convertible securities, which went from 14% to about 7% of net assets on March 31.

As credit spreads widened, we found these securities somewhat less attractive and opted instead to increase exposure to floating-rate loans, which had valuations we found more compelling.

We also added to the fund's exposure to high-yield bonds, which grew from 14% at year-end to roughly 16% of the portfolio as of March 31. The portfolio includes short-duration, high-yielding bonds from companies with low debt.

As we move into the second quarter of 2025, we remain optimistic about our flexibility to invest across the full spectrum of income-oriented asset classes.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s): Adam Kramer Ford O'Neil Ramona Persaud

Trading Symbol: FMSDX

Start Date: September 09, 2015

Size (in millions): $2,799.18

Morningstar Category: Fund Moderately Conservative Allocation

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments all of which are magnified in emerging markets. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk (as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer or counterparty default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks all of which are magnified in emerging markets. Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-quality bonds. Floating rate loans may not be fully collateralized and therefore may decline significantly in value. Moreover, they may be subject to restrictions on resale and sometimes trade infrequently in the secondary market; as a result they may be more difficult to value, buy, or sell. If the fund's asset allocation strategy does not work as intended, the fund may not achieve its objective.

Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you.

CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund; determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.
